sentences.text: What's that?
Yeah, Again?
OK.
This actually what I want to talk about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was in-- not in Plaszow.
This one thing that happened in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that happened in-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] where we worked.
Yeah, that was Plaszow.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Anyhow, we-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] there was-- where the camp was, there was a-- was a factory.
And-- and the-- the men that had to do the work outside, to clean out the barracks and everything, they found-- they found that that was before a factory [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of material.
They made-- they made [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] textile.
And there was lots of wool left.
And the men who worked outside to clean the yard and cleaning that, they would bring in this-- that-- that wool.
And whomever-- some of them had their-- their sisters or brothers or wives there.
So they would give them that wool.
And they would stand to do knitting.
In the factory there, they would-- in the factory there, they would make them needles to knit, to crochet.
Because the men, they would make it.
And then the women could use it.
And they started to knit.
And it was whole business it became, the knitting.
And what they did, they-- and they did a very, very good job.
And they knew how to knit, some of them, you know?
And they would make a nice pullover, for instance, pullover [INAUDIBLE].
And one of them they gave to Schindler's wife.
And she liked it very much.
And he knew that we were using-- they-- they were using the wool from there, you know?
But she liked it very much.
So-- so it was a whole business that-- that crocheting.
So for instance-- Were you there when they presented Mrs.
Schindler with the sweater?
No, no, I wasn't.
I wasn't there.
I mean, I was in the camp.
But I wasn't-- I know someone that worked in the-- in the shop that knew them, you know, knew Schindler.
Some of them, they knew Schindler.
And Schindler knew a number of them.
But for instance, what I-- we-- I remember that at school I learned how to knit socks.
And my grandmother showed me how to make the heel.
[LAUGHS] So I thought I'll try to make the socks, too, and-- and did.
But there was a-- in-- in the coach, there was another woman, an older woman.
And she knew how to do the heel, so she helped me with that.
And I made a pair of socks.
So what did I do with the socks?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The socks I decided to make and to sell.
So-- so how?
But we had no-- I-- no connection.
But I wanted to sell.
So I had-- there was a friend of mine, another friend, that worked in-- in there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the factory.
And I went to him, and I told him I have a pair of socks.
And there came a number of Czechish people came in to work, too.
So I said to him, maybe you can sell these socks to the Czechish people and we-- not so for money, we didn't want money, but for bread.
You know?
And he said, yeah, but how will you-- he say, I'll never forget how he say to me, but how will you get the wool?
I can't buy, because it had to be bought, everything, you know, for something or for money or for bread.
And I can't-- I can't get some money.
And I can't get some bread, because I'm so hungry.
And I have to share the bread-- the bread that they get.
Because we had to buy the wool for bread, see?
And he says, and then when I get the bread, I have to share it with my brother.
Because he-- he's so sick, and I can't take it away from him.
[LAUGHS] So anyhow, we saved the wool.
And we had enough [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wool to make the socks.
And I gave the socks to him.
And he brought the bread, a whole loaf of bread.
And we shared it.
He get half, and I got half.
And I shared it with my cousin.
And he shared it with his brother.
But it helped a lot to have that extra bread.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What else did he say?
When did you leave Plaszow?
Yeah, I was-- I want to tell you another one.
There was a boy from my home town.
There was a boy from my home town.
And I made for him-- I made for him a pullover, like.
I crocheted that, because he-- he was-- he only wear what they give him there in Plaszow.
And he was full of sores and all that, you know?
Because he didn't have anything to treat.
And to-- that.
So anyhow, so I made for him.
I was sorry for him.
I made for him a pullover.
So he was very happy about it.
But then it came the time where they started-- where they started, the people where-- where they came-- the rumors that what they took-- they took away the-- they sent away the people from camps.
They sent them from the east to the west, because they were afraid they will be-- they will be caught.
And German didn't want to-- people to see that they were-- that they were working there [? and ?] [? in ?] [INAUDIBLE].
And so they made them walk.
So he came to me and says, Else, do you mind if I change that pullover for a pair of shoes?
Because without the pullover, with my old pullover, I can walk.
But without shoes if we will have to walk, too-- at that time they were talking about that were-- when-- I'll have to walk.
I need the shoes.
I said, go ahead.
Do it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That is a story about the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] about the shoes.
[INAUDIBLE]
Pardon?
No, I-- I didn't have shoes.
I-- I got this-- the shoes there-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there we got the Dutch shoes, the wooden Dutch.
What the Dutch wear, you know?
That's what I got there.
Well, the Plaszow was when they took us to Auschwitz.
There was a huge-- [INAUDIBLE] a huge-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what was it?
A huge-- a lots of people who they had to-- everybody again in the morning, that was when I was dreaming about my father.
I think I told you that my father said that he will save us.
And that was still in my head in the morning-- [INAUDIBLE] in the morning.
And I said to my cousin, everything will be fine.
We'll get out of here.
And-- and that is what happened.
So the-- so-- I stayed together-- [INAUDIBLE] our husbands were first cousins.
But her husband was taken when they were taking the-- the-- people-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the best known people, the 10 people, they were us.
So that was one of them, was the husband.
He was very active in-- in-- in the ghetto.
So he was taken.
But she, somehow, she found herself another friend.

It was amazing how that went, you know, people who lost their husbands and their-- their mothers or fathers and how they-- how they somehow-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somehow got it over and found someone else.
And so she found a very-- a very close friend she had.
And he was working in-- in the office where they make the lists-- where he made the lists.
We went in-- in that.
So I didn't ask him to put me on the list.
Because I knew if he can, he will.
I won't have to ask him.
And that was the evening before we went to Auschwitz, before the transport went.
He came to my cousin, you know, and said to her that he was able to put me on the list.
I think I told you that.
He was able to-- there was one opening.
And he put me on the list.
But he said, remember, tell her that she will have to go to Auschwitz.
And who-- we knew who goes to Auschwitz.
But you never know who comes out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the-- and the horn was blowing.
And everybody had to get out.
But there was a woman that Schindler had [INAUDIBLE], sent.
Schindler had sent, because we were there already three weeks.
And-- and the-- we didn't get out there from Auschwitz, see?
So the husbands of the wives, they were-- they were after Schindler to see that the women get out.
See?
So he's-- so that's when he sent the woman there with the list.
And that woman came out on the-- there in the yard.
And she was reading the names, who has to-- who should step out.
And I was one of them-- them who was on the list.
And we had-- they-- they called us.
And we had to go out and right away put us to a-- to-- to a-- to car.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Not a car-- we went there-- what did we go?
Went to a car-- it wasn't a car.
Was it the train?
Yeah.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah.
Well, no.
We had to-- on the train, they called our names, and we had to go on the train.
And the same day we were in Auschwitz.
Not in Auschwitz in-- in-- at Schindler's-- Brunnlitz.
It was the same day we were out.
Yeah.
First of all, they took us to-- on the-- on the shower.
We had to be clean.
They are always afraid we are not.
The Jews were dirty people.
And then he-- then-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and the first thing the women asked, the women, they were always asked-- they asked when we came to Auschwitz, where are our children?
Because they took the children away.
And they said that they are-- they will be-- they will be in a camp.
And they are looked after.
And you don't need to worry.
When they took them-- they took the children from Plaszow.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So when the mothers asked where are our children when we came-- so one of the women, I'll never forget how she asked.
You are asking?
You are asking for-- about your children?
You ask where they are?
There are you children.
They showed to the chimney.
And they said to the chimney showed.
That's where your children are.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Yeah, what else?
You arrived in Auschwitz.
Yeah.
We then-- then-- we stayed-- yeah.
And we stayed in bunk beds.
We had the bunk beds, like two in the bunk beds, two.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two on the main and two higher.
Well, yes.
We went to-- we went to-- we had to be cleaned.
We had to go to a bathroom.
And we were scared there, because we thought that they will-- that this our last day, that they will-- you know?
But no, they-- we had to wash.
And we-- and we got new dresses.
The old-- our dresses, our own, were taken away.
And they gave us other dresses, but they didn't fit nothing.
They were [LAUGHS] not made to measure.
They were not made to measure that.
And when-- but we had to wear whatever we got.
And then when we were put on those dresses [INAUDIBLE], some women got frantic to see, you know, what they look like in the dress, or a skirt.
But they never tried or never wore a [INAUDIBLE].
Pardon?
More-- more or less.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't-- I didn't worry about that.
Nothing.
We absolutely, we did nothing.
We-- we were [INAUDIBLE]-- afraid, for instance, to go to the bathroom.
Because you never know with German camps that they can shoot you.
So what we had, we were so many women.
We had-- there were-- everybody was afraid to go to the bathroom.
So we had a pail, a big pail that-- that we used, so we shouldn't have to go to the bathroom.
What were you given to eat?
What I meant before, soups, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and-- but mostly potatoes.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Mostly potatoes, boiled potatoes, not even clean or so.
And there was-- they took a handful with those potatoes and threw them on the-- on the bed, on our couch.
And that is what we got.
Actually, not-- not not special.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not I-- I don't remember.
Oh, there-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there's that story about who tried to get away.
There was a Jewish guy.
And somehow he was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the [NON-ENGLISH] took a liking for him.
And he-- and he looked after him.
And he was a big shot Jewish guy somewhere.
And he could go in and out from the camp and everything.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But I-- I don't remember exactly this story, how that was with the [NON-ENGLISH], that he-- this Jewish guy, he got afraid.
And he tried to get away, he got away.
And he tried to get away from-- from [NON-ENGLISH].
And what [NON-ENGLISH] did him, he-- he made him-- he encouraged him, not personally, he-- but through someone else, encouraged him to get out, to run away and get a passport to go to Hungary.
I think at that time it was a good time to Hungary and to get out there.
And-- and he had everything prepared.
And I think two friends, too, that they should go together and go to Hungary.
At the exit.
He was caught at the exit and asked where he's going.
And they checked that.
And they got the paper.
And they shot him.
He did--
Four were shot.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And-- no, I don't remember now.
But he was shot.
And then he was put on-- on the floor.
And-- and the whole camp, everybody, had to pass by and see and warned what is going to happen if anyone will try to get away.
But I don't remember the name.
There is no name from Krakow, a guy.
Or maybe [INAUDIBLE].
Do you have someone from Plaszow that was in Plaszow?
You'll get a lot there.
I forgot the name.
After Auschwitz, you were transported to Brunnlitz?
Yeah.
By train.
Oh, there was nothing bad.
There was just a normal train.
And they took us too long-- they didn't take too long to get there.
We were very happy to be there.
I don't remember any details, but we were very happy to be there.
You knew we'll be looked after.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Oh, that is when [INAUDIBLE] said that I started to knit.
That was the knitting story.
Till the end of the war, I don't-- [LAUGHS] It was--
It was until the-- when we heard that the Russians are advancing.
So then we [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- we took off and went west.
We could go wherever we wanted.
But we went west.
We didn't want to go.
The most didn't want to go.
[INAUDIBLE] or most of them went west.
Yeah.
I went west, too.
But I went home again after a few weeks I was in Prague.
I went home.
I wanted to see who was there and who survived.
So I went back home again.
And it wasn't easy then to go-- go west again.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah.
Well, I wanted-- you know, I was prepared.
And I wanted to see.
And there was one of my husband's sisters, she survived.
And-- and then there were some other relatives.
They were very good to me.
And I stayed with them, so I wasn't alone.
But nobody wanted to stay under the Russians.
Most of them, they went out.
They wanted to go west.
They-- no, they were-- they were good.
They were good.
I have no complaints.
I don't remember anything.
I say I don't remember.
There are so many other things I would have to say, but I-- right now I'm blank.
I found out what happened to my husband.
They took him away to a-- to a factory to work.
And I still had hope that he survived.
But then one day, we were in a [INAUDIBLE] in Auschwitz.
They-- we had to walk somewhere from one-- from one end to the other somehow.
And while we were waiting and there, there came another group.
Another group of women came by.
And one was calling the other, they are-- today they brought Treblinka here.
And when I heard that, I had no-- no hope anymore.
Because that's where my husband was taken, to the- to a factory to work.
But then they-- that's what they did.
They used him, and then they took them to the chimneys.
So she said to that one room, and it was in our group.
Today, Treblinka came in.
So when she said Treblinka, I lost my hope that my husband will be there.
It's a good question.
I don't know.
[DRINKS] Ah.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
I came there-- I went back-- I'm just thinking-- as I [INAUDIBLE]-- that I went-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] yeah.
Everybody-- I wanted to go to America.
And I had my relatives there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And so everybody after the war, whoever one survivor met another asked what are you doing?
We didn't know what to do with ourself.
So I-- once I'm in the street, and I met a guy that I knew from before the war.
And we, again, we ask what are you doing, and how are you.
And he says, oh, I'm very happy.
I got-- today, I got a-- a visa to go to Mexico.
And when I have the-- you can go to Mexico, I get the transit to the States.
And I have my-- my sister there.
And then I'll be in America.
And then you won't have to worry anymore.
I said, oh, that looks nice.
It sounds nice.
Can you get one visa for me, maybe?
He said, well, I can inquire.
I had to pay for it.
But I say never mind.
If you can get, get me visa.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So he-- next time he went to Warsaw-- that was in Warsaw.
And he knew they're somewhere.
And he comes back.
And he says yes, he can get it for me.
I wish I remember how much we paid.
[LAUGHS] I don't remember now.
And he came back, and he-- and we bought two visas, one for me and for another cousin.
To Los Angeles.
There-- in Los Angeles and San Francisco where my father's family.
And--
Pardon?
He wants something from you.
Yeah.
